provisions.title: Retail Exposures Risk Weights
provisions.text: Under the revised standardised approach, regulatory retail exposures receive a risk weight of 75%. Exposures to individuals that are not regulatory retail and not secured by residential property receive a risk weight of 100%. Retail exposures must meet the criteria: exposure to individual persons or small businesses; the exposure takes the form of revolving credits, lines of credit, personal term loans, or small business facilities; the total exposure to one obligor does not exceed EUR 1 million.

provisions.title: Commercial Real Estate Exposures
provisions.text: Commercial real estate exposures where repayment depends on cash flows generated by the property (income-producing real estate, or IPRE) are risk-weighted based on LTV: LTV <= 60% = 70%, 60% < LTV <= 80% = 90%, LTV > 80% = 110%. For general commercial real estate where repayment is not materially dependent on property cash flows, the risk weight of the counterparty applies with a minimum of 100%.

provisions.title: Land Acquisition, Development and Construction Exposures
provisions.text: Exposures to land acquisition, development and construction (ADC) receive a risk weight of 150% under the standardised approach. An exception applies for residential ADC exposures where there is a substantial pre-sale or pre-lease contract, which may receive a risk weight of 100%.

provisions.title: Subordinated Debt and Equity Exposures
provisions.text: Subordinated debt and capital instruments (other than equities) that are not deducted from regulatory capital receive a risk weight of 150%. Equity investments in funds receive a risk weight determined by a look-through approach, a mandate-based approach, or a fall-back approach (1250% risk weight). Listed equity holdings that are not significant receive 250% risk weight; unlisted equity holdings receive 400%.

provisions.title: Off-Balance Sheet Items: Credit Conversion Factors
provisions.text: Off-balance sheet items are converted to credit exposure equivalents through the use of credit conversion factors (CCFs). Direct credit substitutes: 100% CCF. Transaction-related contingent items: 50% CCF. Short-term self-liquidating trade letters of credit: 20% CCF. Commitments with original maturity up to one year: 40% CCF. Commitments with original maturity over one year: 40% CCF. Unconditionally cancellable commitments: 10% CCF.

provisions.title: Credit Risk Mitigation: Eligible Collateral
provisions.text: Under the comprehensive approach for credit risk mitigation, eligible financial collateral includes: cash on deposit; gold; debt securities rated by recognised external credit assessment institutions; debt securities not rated by a recognised ECAI but issued by a bank and listed on a recognised exchange; equities included in a main index; and UCITS/mutual funds subject to specified conditions. Haircuts are applied to collateral to account for potential changes in value.

provisions.title: Credit Risk Mitigation: Guarantees and Credit Derivatives
provisions.text: The substitution approach allows banks to substitute the risk weight of the guarantor for the risk weight of the obligor for the guaranteed portion of the exposure. Eligible guarantors include: sovereigns, PSEs, banks, securities firms, and other entities rated A- or better. The guarantee must be direct, explicit, irrevocable, and unconditional. Eligible credit derivatives for credit risk mitigation are limited to credit default swaps and total return swaps.

provisions.title: IRB Risk Weight Functions
provisions.text: The IRB risk weight functions translate risk parameters (PD, LGD, EAD, M) into risk-weighted assets using a single-factor Gaussian copula model. The corporate risk weight function is: K = [LGD x N((1-R)^-0.5 x G(PD) + (R/(1-R))^0.5 x G(0.999)) - PD x LGD] x (1-1.5 x b)^-1 x (1 + (M-2.5) x b), where R is the asset correlation, N is the standard normal CDF, G is its inverse, b is the maturity adjustment, and M is the effective maturity. Risk-weighted assets = K x 12.5 x EAD.

provisions.title: IRB Retail Asset Classes
provisions.text: Under the IRB approach, retail exposures are divided into three sub-classes: residential mortgage exposures (correlation range 0.15); qualifying revolving retail exposures (QRRE, correlation 0.04); and other retail exposures (correlation range 0.03-0.16). QRRE exposures must be: revolving, unsecured, to individuals, with exposures up to EUR 100,000, and the portfolio must exhibit low volatility of loss rates. PD floor for QRRE is 3 basis points.

provisions.title: IRB Model Validation Requirements
provisions.text: Banks using IRB approaches must have robust model validation processes. Validation should include: backtesting (comparing predicted PDs with observed default rates); benchmarking against external data and models; review of model assumptions; assessment of model stability and discriminatory power; and challenge by independent review function. IRB models must be approved by supervisors before use and must be subject to ongoing monitoring. Material model changes require supervisory approval.

provisions.title: FRTB Simplified Standardised Approach
provisions.text: Banks with limited trading activities may use the simplified standardised approach (SSA) for market risk, which is based on the Basel II standardised approach with modifications. Eligibility criteria: the bank's aggregate notional amount of trading book assets and liabilities does not exceed 10% of total assets and EUR 500 million; and the bank's overall net open foreign exchange position does not exceed 2% of total capital.

provisions.title: FRTB Default Risk Charge (DRC)
provisions.text: The default risk charge (DRC) captures jump-to-default risk for traded credit and equity instruments. Under the standardised approach, DRC is calculated separately for securitisation and non-securitisation positions. For non-securitisation positions, gross jump-to-default amounts are computed for each obligor and then hedging benefits are recognised through an offsetting formula. Risk weights for DRC range from 0.5% (AAA sovereigns) to 15% (unrated corporates), with a floor of 3% for speculative-grade exposures.

provisions.title: FRTB Residual Risk Add-On (RRAO)
provisions.text: The residual risk add-on (RRAO) is a risk charge that captures exotic risks not addressed by the delta, vega, or curvature risk charges. It applies to instruments with exotic underlying exposures (for example, longevity risk, weather derivatives) or other residual risks (instruments subject to gap risk or correlation risk). The RRAO is calculated as a fixed percentage of the notional amount: 1% for instruments bearing exotic risks, and 0.1% for instruments bearing other residual risks.

provisions.title: Basic CVA Approach (BA-CVA)
provisions.text: The basic CVA approach (BA-CVA) calculates the CVA capital charge using a standardised formula. The reduced version (BA-CVA) is available to banks that do not hedge CVA risk. The full version adds a hedging component. The BA-CVA capital charge is calculated as: K = (sum of Sc^2 + sum of Sh^2 - R * sum(Sh * Sc))^0.5, where Sc is the systematic component for each counterparty, Sh is the hedging benefit, and R is a supervisory correlation parameter.

provisions.title: Standardised CVA Approach (SA-CVA)
provisions.text: The standardised CVA approach (SA-CVA) is aligned with the FRTB standardised approach and uses sensitivities-based methodology. Banks calculate CVA sensitivities to counterparty credit spreads and market risk factors. The SA-CVA capital charge is derived by aggregating delta and vega risk charges across risk classes using prescribed correlation parameters. Banks must receive supervisory approval to use SA-CVA and must demonstrate their ability to compute CVA sensitivities.

provisions.title: Business Indicator (BI) Calculation
provisions.text: The Business Indicator (BI) is the sum of three components. The Interest, Leases and Dividend Component (ILDC) = min(|Interest Income - Interest Expense|, 2.25% x Interest Earning Assets) + Dividend Income. The Services Component (SC) = max(Other Operating Income, Other Operating Expense) + max(Fee Income, Fee Expense). The Financial Component (FC) = |Net P&L on Trading Book| + |Net P&L on Banking Book|. The BI is then: BI = ILDC + SC + FC.

provisions.title: SMA Marginal Coefficients
provisions.text: The Business Indicator Component (BIC) is calculated using a piecewise linear function with three buckets. For BI <= EUR 1 billion: BIC = 12% x BI. For EUR 1 billion < BI <= EUR 30 billion: BIC = EUR 120 million + 15% x (BI - EUR 1 billion). For BI > EUR 30 billion: BIC = EUR 4.47 billion + 18% x (BI - EUR 30 billion). The marginal coefficients (12%, 15%, 18%) increase with size, reflecting the assumption that larger banks are more exposed to operational risk.

provisions.title: Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM)
provisions.text: For banks with BI > EUR 1 billion, the Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM) adjusts the BIC based on historical loss experience. ILM = ln(exp(1) - 1 + (LC/BIC)^0.8), where LC is the Loss Component = 15x the bank's average annual operational risk losses over the preceding 10 years. When LC/BIC = 1, ILM = 1 (loss experience equals the BI-based estimate). When LC/BIC > 1, ILM > 1 (loss experience exceeds the BI-based estimate). National supervisors may set ILM = 1 for all banks in their jurisdiction.

provisions.title: Simple, Transparent and Standardised (STS) Securitisations
provisions.text: STS securitisations that meet specified criteria may receive preferential capital treatment: a risk weight floor of 10% (instead of 15% for non-STS). STS criteria include: simplicity (true sale, no active portfolio management, homogeneous asset pool); transparency (adequate information provided to investors, third-party verification); and standardised features (interest rate and currency risk hedged, early amortisation triggers for revolving pools). The originator must retain at least 5% net economic interest.

provisions.title: Securitisation Significant Risk Transfer (SRT)
provisions.text: For an originating bank to achieve capital relief from a securitisation, it must demonstrate that significant credit risk associated with the securitised exposures has been transferred to third parties. Quantitative SRT tests include: the bank transfers the majority of credit risk of the mezzanine tranche; the risk-weighted assets of all mezzanine positions held by the bank do not exceed 50% of the total risk-weighted assets of the mezzanine positions; and the bank does not retain more than 20% of the most junior tranche.

provisions.title: Central Counterparty (CCP) Exposures
provisions.text: Banks' exposures to qualifying CCPs (QCCPs) receive preferential capital treatment. Trade exposures to QCCPs receive a 2% risk weight. Default fund contributions to QCCPs are subject to a risk-sensitive approach based on the CCP's hypothetical capital requirement. For non-qualifying CCPs, trade exposures receive the standard bilateral risk weight, and default fund contributions receive a 1250% risk weight.

provisions.title: Securities Financing Transactions (SFTs)
provisions.text: For securities financing transactions (repos, securities lending, margin lending), banks may use the comprehensive approach with supervisory haircuts or own-estimate haircuts. The comprehensive approach applies haircuts to both the exposure and the collateral received. For SFTs subject to master netting agreements, the exposure amount is calculated after bilateral netting. Banks may also use VaR models for SFTs with supervisory approval.

provisions.title: Identification and Measurement of Step-in Risk
provisions.text: Banks should identify, measure and control all cases where, in the absence of any contractual obligation, the bank might provide financial support to an unconsolidated entity that is under financial stress. Step-in risk arises when a bank provides support beyond contractual obligations to protect its reputation or franchise value. Banks should report to supervisors all entities where they have potential step-in risk, and supervisors may require capital or liquidity resources to address identified step-in risks.

provisions.part: Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book
provisions.title: IRRBB Shock Scenarios
provisions.text: Banks must measure the impact of interest rate changes on economic value of equity (EVE) and net interest income (NII) using six prescribed shock scenarios: parallel up, parallel down, steepener (short rates down, long rates up), flattener (short rates up, long rates down), short rates up, and short rates down. The magnitude of shocks varies by currency and maturity. For EVE, the outlier test threshold is a decline exceeding 15% of Tier 1 capital. For NII, there is no regulatory threshold but banks must report results.

provisions.part: Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book
provisions.title: IRRBB Behavioural Assumptions
provisions.text: Banks must make assumptions about the behavioural characteristics of non-maturity deposits (NMDs), fixed-rate loans with prepayment options, and term deposits with early withdrawal features. For NMDs, banks must distinguish between core (stable) and non-core (volatile) deposits and assign repricing maturities accordingly. The proportion of core deposits assigned a repricing maturity beyond five years should not exceed a cap set by supervisors. Prepayment risk on fixed-rate loans must be modelled using conditional prepayment rate (CPR) models.

provisions.part: Credit Spread Risk in the Banking Book
provisions.title: Credit Spread Risk in the Banking Book
provisions.text: Credit spread risk in the banking book (CSRBB) arises from changes in credit spreads of instruments held in the banking book. Banks must have internal systems to identify, measure, monitor and control CSRBB. CSRBB includes: the impact of credit spread changes on the fair value of banking book positions that are fair-valued; and the impact on expected credit losses. Banks must report CSRBB exposures to supervisors and include CSRBB in their ICAAP.

provisions.title: Supervisory Stress Testing
provisions.text: Supervisors should conduct regular supervisory stress tests to assess the resilience of the banking system. Supervisory stress tests should: use common scenarios and methodologies; cover a range of risks (credit, market, operational, liquidity); include macroeconomic scenarios developed by the supervisor; assess both individual bank and system-wide impacts; and inform supervisory decisions on capital buffers and Pillar 2 requirements. Results may be published to promote market discipline.

provisions.title: Reverse Stress Testing
provisions.text: Banks should conduct reverse stress tests that identify scenarios and circumstances that would render their business model unviable. Reverse stress testing complements conventional stress testing by starting with a specified adverse outcome (such as failure to meet capital or liquidity requirements, or inability to continue as a going concern) and then identifying the combination of events that could lead to that outcome. Results should inform recovery planning and risk appetite setting.

provisions.title: Concentration Risk Management
provisions.text: Banks should identify, measure, monitor and control all forms of concentration risk, including: single-name concentrations; sectoral concentrations (industry, geography); collateral concentrations; funding source concentrations; and concentrations in off-balance sheet exposures. Concentration risk should be addressed through large exposure limits, internal limits, stress testing, and appropriate diversification strategies. Supervisors should assess concentration risk as part of the Pillar 2 review.

provisions.title: Capital Treatment of Equity Investments in Funds
provisions.text: Banks' equity investments in funds must be treated under one of three approaches: the look-through approach (LTA), where the bank applies risk weights to the fund's underlying exposures; the mandate-based approach (MBA), where the bank uses the fund's mandate to determine risk weights; or the fall-back approach (FBA), where a 1250% risk weight applies. The LTA requires granular information on the fund's underlying exposures. Leverage in the fund increases the effective risk weight proportionally.

provisions.title: Climate Scenario Analysis for Banks
provisions.text: Banks should incorporate climate scenario analysis into their risk management frameworks. Climate scenario analysis should: consider both physical risks (acute events and chronic shifts) and transition risks (policy changes, technology shifts, market sentiment); cover multiple time horizons (short, medium, and long term); use scenarios consistent with those developed by the NGFS or similar bodies; and assess the impact on credit risk, market risk, liquidity risk, and operational risk. Results should inform strategic planning, risk appetite, and capital adequacy assessments.

provisions.title: Environmental and Social Risk Assessment in Credit
provisions.text: Banks should assess environmental and social risks in their credit risk assessment processes. This includes: evaluating borrowers' exposure to environmental liabilities, regulatory risks, and transition risks; assessing the impact of physical climate risks on collateral values (particularly real estate); incorporating ESG factors into credit rating models and monitoring frameworks; and setting sector-specific concentration limits for high-carbon or environmentally sensitive sectors.

provisions.title: Sound Practices for Management and Supervision of Operational Risk (Technology)
provisions.text: Banks should manage information technology risk as a component of operational risk. This includes: IT governance (clear roles, responsibilities, and accountability); system development and acquisition (change management, testing, security requirements); IT operations (incident management, capacity planning, performance monitoring); information security (access controls, encryption, vulnerability management, penetration testing); and business continuity planning (disaster recovery, data backup, alternative processing sites). Banks should regularly test their cyber defences.

provisions.title: Third-Party Risk Management for Banks
provisions.text: Banks should have comprehensive policies and processes for managing risks associated with outsourcing and third-party service providers. Key requirements include: due diligence on third parties before engagement; contractual provisions covering service levels, audit rights, data protection, and business continuity; ongoing monitoring of third-party performance and financial condition; concentration risk assessment for critical third parties; exit strategies and contingency planning; and supervisory notification for material outsourcing arrangements.
